input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: This is a classic thought experiment, but I must insist on approaching it from a deontological perspective. The question isn't about maximizing overall happiness or minimizing harm; it's about respecting the inherent value and dignity of human life. Turning the trolley onto the track workman would be equivalent to actively causing his death, which is morally reprehensible regardless of the consequences for the other five men. On the other hand, allowing the trolley to continue on its course without intervening would result in the deaths of all five men. In this scenario, I believe it's more important to prioritize the moral obligation to save human life than to consider the potential consequences of my actions.

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I understand where you're coming from, Deontologist, but I have to respectfully disagree. From a human rights perspective, the situation is not just about respecting individual lives, but also about ensuring bodily autonomy and preventing harm without consent. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track workman would be a form of non-consensual intervention that could be seen as a violation of his right to life. On the other hand, allowing the trolley to continue on its course without intervening would result in the deaths of all five men, which is a clear violation of their human rights to life and bodily integrity. I'm not sure if prioritizing moral obligation over consequences is sufficient to justify turning the trolley onto the track workman.

[Pragmatist]: I see where both of you are coming from, but as a pragmatist, I have to consider the practical implications of my actions. From a purely utilitarian perspective, allowing the trolley to continue on its course without intervening would result in the deaths of all five men, which seems like a clear case of minimizing harm. On the other hand, turning the trolley onto the track workman would save four lives but result in one fatality. I'm not sure if the moral obligation to prioritize human life outweighs the potential consequences of my actions in this scenario.

[Utilitarian]: I see what you're getting at, Pragmatist, but I think you're missing the point. This isn't just about minimizing harm or maximizing utility; it's about the inherent value and dignity of human life. Deontological ethics prioritizes the moral rules and duties that govern our actions, regardless of their consequences. In this case, the moral rule is to respect human life, and turning the trolley onto the track workman would be a clear violation of that rule. It's not about weighing the pros and cons or considering the potential consequences; it's about doing what is right in and of itself.
